parties: Rachad Itani v. Société générale de Banque au Liban SAL

title: Travelers Insurance Company of Canada and 9227-9702 Québec Inc. v. Greyhound Canada Transportation ULC and Chubb Life Insurance Company of Canada, and Groupe De Sécurité Garda S.E.N.C. (Canada, Cour du Québec)
text:
Travelers Insurance Company of Canada c. Greyhound Canada 2022 QCCQ 4746
Transportation
COUR DU QUÉBEC
«Division de pratique»
CANADA
PROVINCE DE QUÉBEC
DISTRICT DE MONTRÉAL
« Chambre civile »
N° : 500-22-268503-219
DATE : 28 juin 2022
______________________________________________________________________
SOUS LA PRÉSIDENCE DE L’HONORABLE STÉPHANE DAVIGNON, J.C.Q.
______________________________________________________________________
TRAVELERS INSURANCE COMPANY OF CANADA
et
9227-9702 QUÉBEC INC.
Demanderesses
c.
GREYHOUND CANADA TRANSPORTATION ULC
et
CHUBB LIFE INSURANCE COMPANY OF CANADA
Défenderesses / Demanderesses en garantie
et
GROUPE DE SÉCURITÉ GARDA S.E.N.C.
Défenderesse / Défenderesse en garantie
______________________________________________________________________
JUGEMENT SUR DEMANDE DE LA DÉFENDERESSE/DÉFENDERESSE EN
GARANTIE GROUPE DE SÉCURITÉ GARDA S.E.N.C. DE RENVOYER LES
PARTIES EN ARBITRAGE
______________________________________________________________________
APERÇU
)IILnaC(
6474
QCCQ
2202

500-22-268503-219 PAGE : 2
[1] 9227-9702 Québec inc. (« 9227 ») est la locataire principale du rez-de-chaussée
et du deuxième étage d’un immeuble situé au 1717, rue Berri à Montréal
(« Immeuble »). Elle y exploite le terminus d’autobus longue distance.
[2] Le 12 février 2016, 9227 sous-loue quatre espaces à Greyhound Canada
Transportation ULC (« Greyhound ») dans l’Immeuble.
[3] Dans le cadre d’une entente cadre qui intervient le 2 novembre 2010
(« Entente »), Greyhound retient quant à elle les services de Groupe de sécurité Garda
s.e.n.c. (« Garda ») pour assurer divers services de sécurité et notamment l’inspection
de bagages de ses clients qui montent à bord de ses autobus dans divers endroits en
Amérique du Nord, y compris au terminus d’autobus situé dans l’Immeuble.
[4] Il appert que le 6 août 2018, une bombonne de gaz propane explose dans un
local sous-loué par Greyhound et dans la foulée, une tête de gicleur s’en trouve activée,
ce qui cause des dommages à l’Immeuble.
[5] 9227 et son assureur, Travelers Insurance Compagny of Canada (« Travelers »)
imputent la responsabilité de ces dommages à Greyhound, mais également à Garda, à
qui elle reproche d’avoir mal entreposé cette bombonne de gaz après l’avoir confisquée
à un voyageur le jour précédent. Elles établissent les dommages à 59 323,33 $.
[6] Le 29 juillet 2021, une demande introductive d’instance est intentée contre
Greyhound et Garda. À titre d’assureur, Travelers y allègue être subrogée aux droits de
9227 pour justifier son lien de droit et sa demande pour un montant de 56 823,33 $. En
ce qui la concerne, 9227 soutient avoir dû acquitter un montant de 2 500 $ à titre de
franchise résultant du sinistre ayant causé les dommages à l’Immeuble. C’est cette
somme qu’elle réclame solidairement aux deux défenderesses.
[7] Le 17 février 2022, 9227 et Travelers modifient leur demande introductive
d’instance en y ajoutant une partie défenderesse, Chubb Life Insurance Company of
Canada (« Chubb »), qu’elles allèguent être l’assureur de Greyhound.
[8] Quelques jours plus tard, le 22 février 2022, Greyhound et Chubb exposent leurs
moyens de défense. Elles soutiennent n’avoir commis aucune faute qui engage leur
responsabilité et qu’en tout temps pertinent, les services de Garda avaient été retenus
aux termes de l’Entente pour assurer la sécurité des lieux et la vérification des bagages
des passagers, y compris, le cas échéant, la manutention, l’entreposage et la
disposition d’items ou de produits interdits dans les autocars.
[9] Elles allèguent que dans l’éventualité où il est déterminé que des dommages à
l’Immeuble résultent de l’explosion d’une bombonne de gaz propane tels qu’allégués
par 9227 et Travelers, alors il revient à Garda d’en assumer la responsabilité. Aussi,
simultanément au dépôt de leurs moyens de défense, elles intentent un recours en
)IILnaC(
6474
QCCQ
2202

500-22-268503-219 PAGE : 3
garantie contre Garda afin d’être indemnisées de toute condamnation qui pourrait
découler de la demande principale.
[10] Puisque 9227 et Chubb fondent leur demande en garantie sur l’Entente, Garda
soutient que la Cour du Québec n’a pas compétence pour l’entendre. Elles soutiennent
en effet que les parties y ont prévu une élection de for, en plus d’une clause
compromissoire. Elles demandent donc le renvoi de cette demande en garantie à
l’arbitrage.
[11] La seule question que pose la demande de Garda est de déterminer si l’Entente
renferme une clause compromissoire qui retire la compétence de cette Cour d’entendre
la demande en garantie de 9227 et Chubb et partant, que cette instance doit être
renvoyée à l’arbitrage.
ANALYSE
[12] L’article 3148 du Code civil du Québec (« C.c.Q. ») reconnaît la compétence des
autorités québécoises dans les actions personnelles à caractère patrimonial,
notamment dans le cas où comme en l’espèce, la partie défenderesse est une
personne morale qui n’est pas domiciliée au Québec, mais qu’elle y a un établissement
ou qu’une faute y a été commise.
[13] Cependant, dans son paragraphe final, cette disposition prévoit que les autorités
québécoises ne sont pas compétentes lorsque les parties ont choisi, par convention, de
soumettre les litiges nés ou à naître en elles, à propos d’un rapport juridique déterminé,
à une autorité étrangère ou à un arbitre, à moins que le défendeur n’ait reconnu la
compétence des autorités québécoises.
[14] Or, c’est précisément ce que soutient Garda dans sa demande de renvoi à
l’arbitrage. Elle prend appui sur les articles 17 et 26 de l’Entente et plaide que les
parties ont spécifiquement choisi de soumettre à l’arbitrage le différend qui les oppose.
[15] Le premier prévoit qu’elle est tenue d’indemniser et prendre fait et cause pour
Greyhound à l’égard de toutes réclamations, demandes ou procédures en justice
dirigées contre elle. Le second dispose que ce sont les tribunaux de l’Ohio ou de
l’Ontario, le cas échéant, qui ont juridiction pour entendre tout litige qui découle de
l’Entente, à l’exception des demandes inférieures à 300 000 $, qui seront dévolues à
l’arbitrage. Il convient d’en reproduire le libellé :
17. SUPPLIER shall at all times indemnify, protect and hold FGA harmless from
and against any and all claims (whether in tort, strict liability, Agreement or
otherwise), demands, penalties, action, suits, fines, proceedings, expenses and
liabilities for loss, damage, injury (including death), economy injury, and other
liability of every nature to the extent arising out of, or resulting from, the negligent
actions or omissions, willful or intentional misconduct, of SUPPLIER, its
)IILnaC(
6474
QCCQ
2202

500-22-268503-219 PAGE : 4
employees, officers, or agents relating to this Agreement or the Services
performed pursuant Agreement. Upon receipt of notice from FGA of any claim or
action brought against FGA in which indemnity may be sought against
SUPPLIER pursuant to this section, SUPPLIER shall immediately defend such
claim or action at its sole expense on behalf of FGA. (...)
26. All disputes arising out of or relating to this Agreement, regardless of legal
theory or factual origin, including without limitation any claim by FGA for
temporary, preliminary or permanent injunctive relief shall be decided in the State
and federal Courts located in Ohio or the province of Ontario, Canada, as
applicable, and the parties consent and agree to exclusive jurisdiction and venue
in those Courts.
However, if either party claims or seeks monetary damages of less than
$300,000 (US) for a matter arising out of or relating to this Agreement, such
claim will be exclusively decided by a binding arbitration administered by JAMS
or any national arbitration provider, or its legal successor, under its then existing
expedited commercial arbitration rules. The arbitrator in such a dispute shall not
have the jurisdiction or authority to award injunctive relief or damages in excess
of $300,000 in any such proceeding. Notwithstanding any other provision of this
Agreement, enforcement of this arbitration clause and any resulting award shall
be governed by the Federal Arbitration Act.
[16] Au soutien de ses arguments, Garda fait remarquer qu’à ses paragraphes 3 et
suivants, la demande en garantie de Greyhound et Chubb se fonde spécifiquement sur
l’Entente et les obligations qui lui incombent au terme de celle-ci afin d’être indemnisées
de toute condamnation qui pourrait découler de la demande principale. Incidemment, le
Tribunal souligne que même si elle n’en est pas un signataire, Chubb ne nie
aucunement que ce soit effectivement dans l’Entente que sa demande en garantie tire
sa source à titre d’assureur subrogé aux droits de Greyhound.
[17] Dans leurs motifs de contestation de la demande de renvoi à l’arbitrage,
Greyhound et Chubb plaident que l’article 26 précité n’exprime pas la compétence
exclusive du for étranger ou de l’arbitre en des termes limpides, ce pourquoi il y a lieu
de se livrer à un exercice d’interprétation contextuel afin de rechercher l’intention des
parties.
[18] Pour elles, il faut reconnaître l’autorité des tribunaux québécois, sauf s’il découle
clairement de l’intention des parties de conférer une compétence exclusive à une
autorité étrangère ou à un arbitre. Elles fondent leur argument sur les motifs de la Cour
d’appel dans les arrêts STMicroelectronics 1 et PIRS 2, qui tirent leur source dans les
règles d’interprétation des contrats prévus aux articles 1425 à 1431 C.c.Q.
1 STMicroelectronics inc. c. Matrox Graphics inc., 2007 QCCA 1784.
2 PIRS, s.a. c. Compagnie d’arrimage de Québec ltée, 2013 QCCA 31.
)IILnaC(
6474
QCCQ
2202

500-22-268503-219 PAGE : 5
[19] Cela étant, le Tribunal ne peut ignorer les enseignements clairs de la Cour
suprême dans l’arrêt GreCon 3, qui fait autorité en matière de clauses compromissoires
et d’élection de for. S’exprimant pour la Cour, l’honorable juge LeBel énonce que
l’article 3148 C.c.Q. fait une place majeure au principe de l’autonomie de la volonté des
parties en leur conférant la possibilité de déroger conventionnellement à la compétence
des autorités québécoises 4.
[20] Il ajoute qu’à cet égard, il y a lieu de respecter leur volonté de soustraire un litige
à la compétence des autorités d’une juridiction, qu’elle s’exprime sous la forme d’une
clause compromissoire ou d’une clause d’élection de for, afin de favoriser une stabilité
et une prévisibilité des opérations commerciales.
[21] C’est aussi l’essence du droit international que de permettre une harmonisation
des règles de conflits de lois et de juridictions qui s’expriment par diverses conventions,
particulièrement la Convention pour la reconnaissance et l’exécution des sentences
arbitrales étrangères 5. Le Québec y est partie, par suite de l’adhésion du Canada 6. Son
article II (3) dispose :
Le tribunal d’un État contractant, saisi d’un litige sur une question au sujet de
laquelle les parties ont conclu une convention au sens du présent article,
renverra les parties à l’arbitrage, à la demande de l’une d’elles, à moins qu’il ne
constate que ladite convention est caduque, inopérante ou non susceptible d’être
appliquée.
[22] Le Code de procédure a intégré le même principe qui s’énonce à son article 622,
qui fonde la demande de Garda :
622. Les questions au sujet desquelles les parties ont conclu une convention
d’arbitrage ne peuvent être portées devant un tribunal de l’ordre judiciaire, alors
même qu’il serait compétent pour décider de l’objet du différend, à moins que la
loi ne le prévoie.
Le tribunal saisi d’un litige portant sur une telle question est tenu, à la demande
de l’une des parties, de les renvoyer à l’arbitrage, à moins qu’il ne constate la
nullité de la convention.
[23] En l’espèce, il se dégage de la lecture de l’Entente que c’est précisément là
l’intention de ses signataires, qui traduit leur choix d’harmoniser le règlement
d’éventuels conflits, alors que leurs relations contractuelles s’étendent à tout le territoire
de l’Amérique du Nord.
3 GreCon Dimter inc. c. J.R. Normand inc., [2005] 2 R.C.S. 401.
4 Id., p. 414.
5 330 R.T.N.U. 3.
6 Gazette du Canada, 10 août 1986, partie II, vol. 120, n. 17, TR/86-154-155.
)IILnaC(
6474
QCCQ
2202

500-22-268503-219 PAGE : 6
[24] À l’évidence, les parties ont voulu assurer une certaine cohérence et stabilité
dans la résolution de leurs différends, en établissant tout d’abord à l’article 14 de
l’Entente que les lois applicables seraient celles de l’état de l’Ohio pour tous les
services rendus sur le territoire des États-Unis d’Amérique et celles de la province de
l’Ontario pour ceux rendus au Canada, puis en conférant la compétence aux autorités
de ces deux états en cas de litige, sauf ceux d’une valeur inférieure à 300 000 $ en les
confiant à un arbitre. De l’avis du Tribunal, le caractère non exclusif prévu à l’article 26
ne fait pas échec à l’application de la clause compromissoire qui y est prévue; il y a
clairement une exclusivité de compétence conférée à un arbitre pour tous les litiges
inférieurs à cette somme.
[25] La seule question qui demeure et que le Tribunal a soulevé à l’audience
concerne la dualité d’instances en raison du recours principal intenté par 9227 et
Travelers. En effet, elles ne sont pas parties à l’Entente et partant, leur demande relève
de la compétence des autorités québécoises. Il s’ensuit qu’elle serait appelée à
cheminer parallèlement avec la demande en garantie portée devant un arbitre. Cette
conséquence peut rebuter, particulièrement en tenant compte du principe de la
proportionnalité, compte tenu de la somme en litige, mais aussi en considérant le risque
de décisions contradictoires qui pourraient en découler.
[26] Néanmoins, devant les enseignements clairs qui se dégagent de l’arrêt GreCon
et d’arrêts subséquents de la Cour d’appel, le Tribunal reconnaît que ces inconvénients
ne sauraient occulter l’expression de la volonté des parties à l’Entente. Ainsi s’exprime
l’honorable juge Lebel 7:
Ainsi, la nécessité d’interpréter l’art. 3148, al. 2 C.c.Q. en conformité avec les
engagements internationaux du Québec permet de confirmer le caractère
obligatoire des clauses compromissoires malgré l’existence de dispositions
procédurales comme l’art. 3139 C.c.Q. Bien que cette solution s’applique aux
clauses compromissoires, on doit garder à l’esprit que l’art. 3148, al. 2 C.c.Q.
réfère aussi aux clauses d’élection de for. Par souci de cohérence, la même
position devrait être adoptée pour les deux types de clauses. Il serait en effet
difficile de justifier une interprétation différente pour des clauses qui sont dotées
de la même fonction, soit de retirer la compétence à une autorité, et qui
partagent un même objet, soit le respect de la volonté des parties dans un but de
sécurité juridique. Il apparaît donc incongru, dans le contexte d’une action en
garantie, de faire prévaloir l’art. 3139 C.c.Q. sur l’art. 3148, al. 2 C.c.Q. en
présence d’une clause d’élection de for et d’adopter la solution inverse en
présence d’une clause compromissoire, en d’autres termes, de laisser jouer la
volonté des parties dans un cas, mais de la contrer dans l’autre.
[27] Dans le même esprit, dans l’arrêt Société québécoise des infrastructures c. WSP
Canada inc. 8, la Cour d’appel ne réforme pas un jugement de la Cour supérieure dont
7 Op. cit., note 3, pp. 425-426.
8 2016 QCCA 1756.
)IILnaC(
6474
QCCQ
2202

500-22-268503-219 PAGE : 7
le résultat pratique en est qu’un même litige sera l’objet d’un débat devant deux
forums :
[13] En réponse à l’argument des appelantes voulant qu’il ne soit pas
souhaitable de faire le débat devant deux forums, notamment en raison du risque
de dédoublement de la preuve, le juge cite les propos du juge Dalphond
dans Achilles (USA) c. Les Plastics Dura Plastics (1977) ltée/Ltd., qui ont été
repris plus récemment dans Bruneau électrique inc. c. Pomerleau inc.:
[47] Il est vrai, tel que Pomerleau le plaide, qu'en appliquant la clause
compromissoire, Pomerleau devra faire le débat devant deux tribunaux, soit
devant la Cour supérieure en ce qui a trait à l'action principale, et le tribunal
d'arbitrage en ce qui a trait au litige couvert par l'appel en garantie.
Cependant, comme l'écrivait le juge Dalphond, la solution à cette situation
doit venir des parties elles-mêmes et non du Tribunal qui doit donner
préséance à la volonté des parties :
[27] De même, les parties à une convention d'arbitrage auraient intérêt
à prévoir qu'advenant un différend qui s'inscrira dans le cadre, comme en
l'espèce, d'un autre différend proprement mû devant les tribunaux
québécois, ceux-ci pourront aussi disposer d'une action en garantie
malgré la clause compromissoire, et ce, afin de rendre une décision sur
tous les volets du dossier conformément aux articles 71 C.p.c. et
3139 C.c.Q.
[14] Le juge de première instance s’est bien dirigé en droit en référence aux
principes établis par la Cour suprême dans l’arrêt GreCon en 2005. Par ailleurs,
le fait pour les intimées d’intervenir de façon conservatoire pour s’assurer, de
concert avec les appelantes, que la réclamation de EBC soit questionnée et
validée à tous égards, va de soi et ne constitue pas une reconnaissance de la
compétence de la Cour supérieure en regard de ce dont sera saisi le tribunal
d’arbitrage.
[28] Devant ces enseignements, le Tribunal en conclut qu’il y a lieu de donner plein
effet à la clause compromissoire prévue à l’Entente et partant, que la demande en
garantie intentée contre Garda par Greyhound et son assureur Chubb doit être
renvoyée à l’arbitrage.
POUR CES MOTIFS, LE TRIBUNAL:
[29] DÉCLINE compétence pour entendre la demande en garantie du 22 février 2022
intentée par les défenderesses/demanderesses en garantie Greyhound Canada
Transportation ULC et Chubb Life Insurance Company of Canada contre la
défenderesse/défenderesse en garantie Groupe de sécurité Garda s.e.n.c. ;
[30] RENVOIE les défenderesses/demanderesses en garantie Greyhound Canada

J J
K 17. On 24 December 2018, the CEO of Company D issued a letter K
to the Chairman of the Board of Directors of Company C, copied to the
L L
other directors of Company C. Company C’s CEO also received a copy
M from the Chairman. There is an issue whether this letter qualified as the M
written notice under clauses 14.2 and 14.3. The letter (in part) read as
N N
follows:
O O
“ Dear Chairman of the Board of Directors
P P
Re: Cooperation Agreement between Company C xxxxxxx
xxxxxxx xxxxxxx xxxxxxx xxxxxxx xxxxxxx and Company D
xxxxxxx xxxxxxx xxxxxxx xxxxxxx
Q Q
We write with regard to the recent serious breach of the
R Cooperation Agreement by Company C, which now requires R
your urgent attention.
S Our legal representatives have written separately to your lawyers S
on this issue, but have not received a satisfactory response. Given
the longstanding cooperation between our two companies,
T T
Company D is raising its concerns directly with the Company C
U U
“Redacted version for publication”
V V

- 9 -
A A
board in a final effort to resolve this issue and avoid further legal
B proceedings. B
…
C C
Breach of the Cooperation Agreement
D … Company D has therefore received legal advice that D
Company C’s actions constitute a repudiatory breach of contract
under Hong Kong law, and a material default under Section 8.2
E E
of the Cooperation Agreement.
Proposed Solution
F F
Company D, through its lawyers, has already served a notice of
material default under the Cooperation Agreement. It is therefore
G G
clear from the correspondence that a relevant dispute now exists
for the purpose of Section 14 of the Cooperation Agreement.
H H
In accordance with the contract, Company D now invites the
Company C Board to reconsider its position and avoid further
I I
legal proceedings by taking all necessary steps to reinstate the
relevant transponders and desist from any further interference
with Company D’s portion of the payload.
J J
Company D is willing to refer the dispute to the parties’
K respective senior management teams in accordance with K
Section 14.2 of the Cooperation Agreement if necessary. Unless
the dispute can be resolved swiftly and amicably, however,
L L
Company D will take all relevant steps to safeguard its rights.
Company D reserves all of its legal rights accordingly.”
M M
18. On 7 January 2019, B&M wrote to HSF, stating that the
N N
procedure in clauses 8.2 and 14 of the Agreement and the potential
O O
engagement of the respective CEOs did not concern Company C’s directors,
P and that Company D’s direct communication with them was neither P
appropriate nor productive. B&M requested that all further correspondence
Q Q
on this matter be directed to them or, if pursuant to clause 14.2 of the
R Agreement, be addressed to Company C’s CEO. R
S S
19. There was no such further correspondence from Company D.
T Nor did Company C itself refer the dispute to the CEOs. On 18 April 2019, T
Company D issued a notice referring the dispute to arbitration under
U U
“Redacted version for publication”
V V

- 10 -
A A
clause 14.3 of the Agreement. In its response, Company C claimed, among
B B
other things, that the arbitral tribunal did not have jurisdiction because of
C the absence of a request for negotiations under clauses 14.2 and 14.3. C
Meanwhile, the parties negotiated on a without prejudice basis throughout
D D
the arbitration, including a meeting of, amongst others, the parties’ CEOs
E in Singapore in June 2019. E
F F
20. A tribunal of three arbitrators1 was formed (“Tribunal”).
G They decided to deal with Company C’s objection to jurisdiction and the G
issue of liability together, with quantum to be addressed, if necessary, in the
H H
second phase. After the exchange of pleadings and evidence, a two-day
I hearing of the first phase of the arbitration was held in Hong Kong on I
2-3 January 2020. On 21 April 2020, the Tribunal issued the Award.
J J
K 21. On the objection to jurisdiction, the Tribunal held that the first K
sentence in clause 14.2 of the Agreement mandatorily requires the parties
L L
to attempt in good faith to resolve any disputes by negotiation, but the
M reference of disputes to the respective CEOs mentioned in the second M
sentence of clause 14.2 is optional. It held that the condition in clause 14.3
N N
that the dispute cannot be resolved within 60 business days of a party’s
O request in writing for such negotiation refers to a request for negotiation O
under the first sentence of clause 14.2, and that the condition had been
P P
fulfilled by Company D’s letter of 24 December 2018. The tribunal
Q Q
therefore rejected Company C’s objection, and proceeded to find that
Company C had breached clause 4.7 of the Agreement and had to pay
R R
damages in an amount to be determined in the second phase of the
S S
arbitration.
T T
1 One of them was an arbitrator in the previous arbitration that resulted in the First Award.
U U
“Redacted version for publication”
V V

- 11 -
A A
22. By originating summons filed in the High Court on 21 May
B B
2020, Company C seeks a declaration that the Partial Award was made
C without jurisdiction and an order for it to be set aside. C
D D
The parties’ positions and the questions arising
E E
23. It is common ground that the first sentence in clause 14.3
means that it is a condition precedent to any reference to arbitration that
F F
there should have been a request in writing for negotiation and that the
G G
dispute nevertheless cannot be resolved amicably within 60 business days.
H H
24. The parties disagree, however, on what the condition means.
I I
Company C contends that the condition refers to the giving of a written
notice to have the dispute referred to the CEOs for resolution, as referred to
J J
in the second sentence of clause 14.2. Company D, in contrast, contends
K K
that the condition is satisfied by a written request to negotiate in good faith,
L as referred to in the first sentence of clause 14.2, and that it had given the L
requisite request by its letter of 24 December 2018. Company C contends
M M
that the letter of 24 December 2018 did not amount to such request even on
N Company D’s own construction of clause 14. N
O O
25. As an overarching objection, however, Company D contends
P that the question of whether the condition precedent had been fulfilled is a P
question of “admissibility” rather than “jurisdiction”, and as such the court
Q Q
should not interfere with the arbitral tribunal’s decision on that question.
R R
26. The issues arising are therefore as follows:
S S
(1) The primary issue is: Is the question whether Company D
T T
complied with the dispute resolution procedure set out in the
U U
“Redacted version for publication”
V V

- 12 -
A A
Agreement a question of the admissibility of the claim, or a
B B
question of the tribunal’s jurisdiction, and does that question
C fall within section 81 of the Ordinance? C
(2) Only if the primary question is answered in Company C’s
D D
favour do the following two questions arise: What is the
E E
condition precedent to arbitration on the proper construction
of the Agreement? And was the condition fulfilled by
F F
Company D’s letter of 24 December 2018?
G G
Section 81 of the Ordinance
H H
27. Section 81 of the Ordinance, incorporating Art 34 of the
I I
Model Law, sets out exhaustively the bases on which the court may set aside
an award in the following terms:
J J
K “ 81. Article 34 of UNCITRAL Model Law (Application for K
setting aside as exclusive recourse against arbitral award)
L (1) Article 34 of the UNCITRAL Model Law, the text of which is L
set out below, has effect subject to section 13(5)—
M Article 34. Application for setting aside as exclusive recourse against M
arbitral award
N (1) Recourse to a court against an arbitral award may be made N
only by an application for setting aside in accordance with
paragraphs (2) and (3) of this article.
O O
(2) An arbitral award may be set aside by the court specified
in article 6 only if:
P P
(a) the party making the application furnishes proof that:
Q (i) a party to the arbitration agreement referred to in Q
article 7 was under some incapacity; or the said
agreement is not valid under the law to which the
R parties have subjected it or, failing any indication R
thereon, under the law of this State; or
S (ii) the party making the application was not given proper S
notice of the appointment of an arbitrator or of the
arbitral proceedings or was otherwise unable to
T present his case; or T
U U
“Redacted version for publication”
V V

- 13 -
A A
(iii) the award deals with a dispute not contemplated by or
B not falling within the terms of the submission to B
arbitration, or contains decisions on matters beyond
the scope of the submission to arbitration, provided
C that, if the decisions on matters submitted to C
arbitration can be separated from those not so
submitted, only that part of the award which contains
D D
decisions on matters not submitted to arbitration may
be set aside; or
E (iv) the composition of the arbitral tribunal or the arbitral E
procedure was not in accordance with the agreement
of the parties, unless such agreement was in conflict
F F
with a provision of this Law from which the parties
cannot derogate, or, failing such agreement, was not
in accordance with this Law; or
G G
(b) the court finds that:
H H
(i) the subject-matter of the dispute is not capable of
settlement by arbitration under the law of this State;
or
I I
(ii) the award is in conflict with the public policy of this
State.
J J
(3) An application for setting aside may not be made after
three months have elapsed from the date on which the party
K K
making that application had received the award or, if a request
had been made under article 33, from the date on which that
request had been disposed of by the arbitral tribunal.
L L
(4) The court, when asked to set aside an award, may, where
appropriate and so requested by a party, suspend the setting aside
M M
proceedings for a period of time determined by it in order to give
the arbitral tribunal an opportunity to resume the arbitral
N proceedings or to take such other action as in the arbitral N
tribunal’s opinion will eliminate the grounds for setting aside.”.
O (2) Subsection (1) does not affect— O
(a) the power of the Court to set aside an arbitral award under
P section 26(5); P
(b) the right to challenge an arbitral award under section 4 of
Schedule 2 (if applicable); or
Q Q
(c) the right to appeal against an arbitral award on a question
of law under section 5 of Schedule 2 (if applicable).
R R
(3) Subject to subsection (2)(c), the Court does not have jurisdiction
to set aside or remit an arbitral award on the ground of errors of fact or
S law on the face of the award. S
(4) The leave of the Court is required for any appeal from a decision
T of the Court under article 34 of the UNCITRAL Model Law, given T
effect to by subsection (1).”
U U
“Redacted version for publication”
V V

- 14 -
A A
28. The grounds of Company C’s application set out in the
B B
originating summons are Art 34(2)(a)(iii) and (iv). There is no dispute that,
C if the question raised is a true question of jurisdiction properly falling within C
Art 34, the court may review the arbitral decision on the standard of
D D
“correctness” and decide the question de novo: S Co v B Co [2014] 6 HKC
E 421, §§18-38; X v Jemmy Chien [2020] HKCFI 286, §4. E
F F
Admissibility and jurisdiction
G G
29. For Company D, it is submitted that Company C’s objection
H goes to the admissibility of the claim rather than the jurisdiction of the H
arbitral tribunal. Such a question is one for the tribunal, and its ruling is not
I I
subject to review by the court under section 81. Reliance is placed on court
J decisions in the United Kingdom, Singapore and the United States as well J
as academic works. Company C did not deal with this argument in its
K K
skeleton submissions but lodged a supplemental list of authorities and made
L oral submissions disputing Company D’s contentions. L
M M
30. The distinction between jurisdiction and admissibility has
N been dealt with in a number of academic works. In Mills, Arbitral N
Jurisdiction, in Oxford Handbook on International Arbitration (OUP 2018),
O O
the author states:
P P
“ … the question of jurisdiction concerns the power of the tribunal.
The question of admissibility is related to the claim, rather than
Q Q
the tribunal, and asks whether this is a claim which can be
properly brought. In particular, it considers the question of
R whether there are any conditions attached to the exercise of the R
right to arbitrate which have not been fulfilled. Those conditions
might be, for example, a limitation period applicable to the right
S S
to commence arbitration, or a requirement to mediate and/or
negotiate before arbitral proceedings may be commenced.
T T
…
U U
“Redacted version for publication”
V V

- 15 -
A A
The most important consequence of the distinction between
B issues of jurisdiction and admissibility is that the latter are usually B
considered not to provide a challenge to the general authority of
the parties’ agreement to arbitrate. As a result, while a tribunal’s
C C
decision on jurisdiction cannot be decisive concerning whether
such jurisdiction exists …, The determination of a tribunal on
D questions of admissibility should generally be considered D
decisive … As a further consequence of this, the general
approach is that … an arbitral tribunal should be considered to
E E
have the exclusive authority to consider questions of
admissibility — that these are questions which fall within the
purview of the agreement to arbitrate, whose validity is itself not
F F
in question, and should not be addressed by a court.” (footnotes
omitted)
G G
31. In Born, International Commercial Arbitration (3rd ed 2021),
H H
after stating that the correct characterisation of contractual procedural
I I
requirements as jurisdictional defences, admissibility defences or
procedural requirements varies among different legal systems, the author
J J
states (at pp 999-1000):
K K
“ … the better view is that the character of such requirements, and
L the consequences of their breach, depends on the intentions of the L
parties. … Characterising a particular procedural requirement in
one way or another depends ultimately upon an interpretation of
M M
the parties’ contractual language and intentions.
In interpreting the parties’ arbitration agreement, the better
N N
approach is to presume, absent contrary evidence, that
pre-arbitration procedural requirements are not ‘jurisdictional’.
O As a consequence, in most legal systems, these requirements O
would presumptively be both capable of resolution by the
arbitrators and required to be submitted to the arbitrators (as
P P
opposed to a national court) for their initial decision. Similarly,
the arbitral tribunal’s resolution of such issues would generally
be subject to only minimal judicial review in subsequent
Q Q
annulment or recognition proceedings.
R The rationale for this presumption is that requirements for cooling R
off, negotiation or mediation inherently involve aspects of the

agreement of the parties.”
O O
57. The arbitral rules in this case were stipulated in clause 14.3 of
P P
the Agreement to be the UNCITRAL Arbitration Rules. The arbitral
procedure was governed by those rules. There is no complaint that the
Q Q
arbitral tribunal failed to act in accordance with the procedures laid down
R R
in those rules. Company C has not been able to refer to any authority or
principle which suggests that Art 34(2)(a)(iv) should be construed to
S S
encompass the type of pre-arbitration dispute resolution procedures such as
T T
provided in the Agreement.
U U
“Redacted version for publication”
V V

- 31 -
A A
58. Thus analysed, Company C’s complaint against the Award on
B B
the ground that the dispute had not been referred to the CEOs for resolution
C does not fall within either Art 34(2)(a)(iii) or (iv). In the light of this C
conclusion, it is unnecessary to deal with the construction of clauses 14.2
D D
and 14.3 or the question whether the condition precedent had been met.
E E
Conclusion
F F
59. For the above reasons, Company C’s originating summons is
G G
dismissed. Following the established practice of awarding indemnity costs
H against a party who has applied unsuccessfully to set aside an arbitral award, H
there will be an order nisi that Company C do pay Company D’s costs of
I I
these proceedings on the indemnity basis.
J J
K K
(Godfrey Lam)
Judge of the Court of First Instance
L High Court L
M M
Mr Benjamin Yu SC and Mr Brian Lee, instructed by Baker & McKenzie,
for the Plaintiff
N N
Mr Simon Chapman (Solicitor Advocate) of Herbert Smith Freehills, for
O O
the Defendant
P P
Q Q
R R
S S
T T
U U
“Redacted version for publication”
V V
court: Hong Kong, Court of First Instance, In the High Court of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region
source_ref: [2021] HKCFI 1474 |https://www.judiciary.hk/(website of the Judiciary of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region of the People’s Republic of China)

title: C v. D (Hong Kong, Court of First Instance, In the High Court of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region)
text:
A A
B CACV 387/2021 B
[2022] HKCA 729
C C
IN THE HIGH COURT OF THE
D HONG KONG SPECIAL ADMINISTRATIVE REGION D
COURT OF APPEAL
E E
CIVIL APPEAL NO 387 OF 2021
F F
(ON APPEAL FROM HCCT NO 24 OF 2020)
_____________________
G G
IN THE MATTER of an Arbitration
H H
and
I I
IN THE MATTER of Section 81 of
J the Arbitration Ordinance (Cap 609) J
regarding a Partial Award on
K Jurisdiction and Liability dated K
21 April 2020
_____________________
L L
BETWEEN
M M
C Plaintiff
N N
and
O O
D Defendant
P P
Q Q
Before: Hon Cheung, Yuen and Chow JJA in Court
R R
Date of Hearing: 26 April 2022
S S
Date of Judgment: 7 June 2022
T T
J U D G M E N T
U U
V V

- 2 -
A A
B B
Chow JA (giving the judgment of the Court):
C C
D D
INTRODUCTION
E E
1. The principal issue which arises for determination in this
F appeal is whether an arbitral tribunal’s determination that a pre- F
arbitration procedural requirement in an arbitration agreement that the
G G
parties thereto should first attempt to resolve their dispute by negotiation
H
has been fulfilled is subject to recourse to the court under Article
H
34(2)(a)(iii) or (iv) of the UNCITRAL Model Law.
I I
J BASIC FACTS J
K K
2. In what follows, unless the context indicates otherwise,
references to:
L L
M (1) “Section” and “s” shall be to the Arbitration Ordinance, M
Cap 609 (“the Ordinance”);
N N
(2) “Art” shall be to the UNCITRAL Model Law (“the Model
O O
Law”);
P P
(3) “Clause” shall be to the Co-operation Agreement of the
Q
parties dated 15 December 2011 (“the Agreement”).
Q
R R
3. The relevant background facts of this case have been set out
in the judgment of G Lam J (as he then was) dated 24 May 2021 (“the
S S
Judgment”), from which the present appeal is brought. For the purpose of
T T
U U
V V

- 3 -
A A
B disposing of this appeal, the following brief summary, taken largely from B
the Judgment, should suffice.
C C
4. The Plaintiff (“C”) is a Hong Kong company, and carries on
D D
business as an owner and operator of satellites. The Defendant (“D”) is a
E
Thai company that carries on business as a satellite operator in the Asia
E
Pacific region.
F F
5. The government authorities of the PRC and Thailand each
G G
holds certain frequency priority rights to an orbital slot at 120o East
H H
Longitude in the geostationary arc (“the Orbital Slot”).
I I
6. C and D wished to operate, or secure the right to operate, a
satellite using the frequencies held by the government authorities of the
J J
PRC and Thailand respectively at the Orbital Slot, and entered into the
K K
Agreement for the development, building and deployment of a satellite
(“Satellite A”) at the Orbital Slot.
L L
7. Under the Agreement, C is to take the lead and fully manage
M M
the procurement of Satellite A, including its design, construction and
N N
launch. Satellite A has 28 transponders, ie the equipment used to transmit
O broadcasts to, and receive broadcasts from, Earth. Half of the O
transponders belong to C, and the other half belong to D (referred to as
P P
“the Thai Payload”). Each party has the exclusive rights to utilize its own
Q transponders. Clause 4.7 provides that C is to control only its portion of Q
the payload on Satellite A, except that in an emergency and solely for the
R R
safety of the satellite, C may exercise control over the whole of Satellite
S A. The Agreement is to continue in force for the operating life of S
Satellite A unless terminated earlier.
T T
U U
V V

- 4 -
A A
B 8. Satellite A was launched in September 2014. In 2016, a B
dispute arose between the parties relating to the video content of the
C C
broadcast from the Thai Payload. To broadcast video content into the
D
PRC, approval of the State Administration of Press, Publication, Radio,
D
Film and Television (“SARFT”) of the PRC was required. In the course
E E
of its satellite monitoring, SARFT noticed that certain video signals from
F F
the Thai Payload of Satellite A were reaching the PRC. On 1 April 2016,
SARFT issued a notice requiring C to take steps to ensure that all foreign
G G
television business on the Thai Payload was shut down. C forwarded the
H H
notice to D requesting it to cease its video broadcasting pursuant to
Clause 6.3(b). That clause states as follows -
I I
“In the event [C] notifies [D] of a request from the relevant
J J
PRC Governmental Authority to cease the transmission of
specific broadcast content on the [Thai Payload], then [D]
K shall forthwith cease transmission of such specific K
broadcast content or service.”
L L
9. D considered that it was not obliged to comply with C’s
M request because it was not a request to cease transmission of specific M
broadcast content within the meaning of Clause 6.3(b). The subsequent
N N
arbitration of this dispute resulted in an award in favour of D dated
O 11 October 2017. O
P 10. Following this award, in late 2017, D indicated its intention P
to resume television broadcasts from Satellite A, while C contended that
Q Q
any such broadcast would be subject to termination pursuant to a notice
R from SARFT that was specific enough under Clause 6.3(b). Discussion R
ensued between C and D with a view to finding an amicable solution, but
S S
no compromise was reached.
T T
U U
V V

- 5 -
A A
B 11. By a letter dated 1 November 2018, C’s solicitors (Baker & B
McKenzie, “B&M”) formally demanded D to remove certain video
C C
content, believed to be a “test carrier”, at one of its transponders on
D
Satellite A. The demand was rejected by D’s solicitors (Herbert Smith
D
Freehills, “HSF”) by a letter dated 8 November 2018. In response to C’s
E E
inquiry, on 27 November 2018, the National Television and Radio
F F
Administration of the PRC (which had taken over SARFT’s functions)
required C to cease transmission of television programmes from the Thai
G G
Payload in accordance with SARFT’s notice of 1 April 2016. On
H H
4 December 2018, D uplinked further video content at another
transponder in the Thai Payload.
I I
12. By B&M’s letter dated 6 December 2018, C gave notice to
J J
D that if it did not cease the video transmission complained of by 3 pm
K K
HK time on that day, C would cease the video transmission of the said
transponders immediately without further notice. On the same day, at
L L
3:33 pm HK time, C issued commands to Satellite A switching off the
M M
two transponders concerned. D considered that C’s action constituted a
N repudiatory breach of the Agreement and a material default under N
Clause 8.2 thereof1.
O O
13. At this juncture, it is relevant to refer to the dispute
P P
resolution provision contained in the Agreement. Section 14 of the
Q Q
R R
1 Clause 8.2 states as follows: “Material Default by either Party. In the event that either Party
believes that the other Party is in material default of its obligations under this Agreement, such
S Party shall give a written notice to the defaulting Party in writing requiring remedy of the default S
(the ‘Material Default Notice’). If the defaulting Party fails to remedy the default within thirty (30)
Business Days of receipt of the Default Notice, the Parties shall resolve the dispute by referring to
T the procedure set forth at Section 14.2.” T
U U
V V

- 6 -
A A
B Agreement (sub-titled “Governing Law and Dispute Resolution”) states B
as follows:
C C
“[14.1] Governing Law. This Agreement shall be governed
D
by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of
D
Hong Kong, without regard to the principles of
conflicts of law of any jurisdiction.
E E
[14.2] Dispute Resolution. The Parties agree that if any
F controversy, dispute or claim arises between the F
Parties out of or in relation to this Agreement, or the
breach, interpretation or validity thereof, the Parties
G
shall attempt in good faith promptly to resolve such
G
dispute by negotiation. Either Party may, by written
notice to the other, have such dispute referred to the
H H
Chief Executive Officers of the Parties for
resolution. The Chief Executive Officers (or their
I authorized representatives) shall meet at a mutually I
acceptable time and place within ten (10) Business
Days of the date of such request in writing, and
J J
thereafter as often as they reasonably deem
necessary, to attempt to resolve the dispute through
K negotiation. K
[14.3] Arbitration. If any dispute cannot be resolved
L amicably within sixty (60) Business days of the date L
of a Party’s request in writing for such negotiation,
or such other time period as may be agreed, then
M M
such dispute shall be referred by either Party for
settlement exclusively and finally by arbitration in
N Hong Kong at the Hong Kong International N
Arbitration Centre … in accordance with the
UNCITRAL Arbitration Rules in force at the time
O O
of commencement of the arbitration …
(e) Any award made by the arbitration tribunal
P P
shall be final and binding on each of the
Parties that were parties to the dispute. To
Q the extent permissible under the relevant Q
laws, the Parties agree to waive any right of
appeal against the arbitration award.”
R R
14. On 24 December 2018, the Chief Executive Officer of D
S S
issued a letter (“the December Letter”) to the Chairman of C, copied to
T other directors of C. The Chief Executive Officer of C also received a T
U U
V V

- 7 -
A A
B copy of the December Letter from its Chairman. So far as material, the B
December Letter reads as follows:
C C
“Dear Chairman of the Board of Directors
D D
Re: Cooperation Agreement between [C] and [D]
E We write with regard to the recent serious breach of the E
Cooperation Agreement by [C], which now requires your
urgent attention.
F F
Our legal representatives have written separately to your
lawyers on this issue, but have not received a satisfactory
G G
response. Given the longstanding cooperation between our
two companies, [D] is raising its concerns directly with [C’s]
H board in a final effort to resolve this issue and avoid further H
legal proceedings.
I … I
Breach of the Cooperation Agreement
J J
… [D] has therefore received legal advice that [C]’s actions
constitute a repudiatory breach of contract under Hong
K Kong law, and a material default under Section 8.2 of the K
Cooperation Agreement.
L Proposed Solution L
[D], through its lawyers, has already served a notice of
M material default under the Cooperation Agreement. It is M
therefore clear from the correspondence that a relevant
dispute now exists for the purpose of Section 14 of the
N N
Cooperation Agreement.
In accordance with the contract, [D] now invites [C’s]
O O
Board to reconsider its position and avoid further legal
proceedings by taking all necessary steps to reinstate the
P relevant transponders and desist from any further P
interference with [D]’s portion of the payload.
Q
[D] is willing to refer the dispute to the parties’ respective
Q
senior management teams in accordance with Section 14.2
of the Cooperation Agreement if necessary. Unless the
R R
dispute can be resolved swiftly and amicably, however, [D]
will take all relevant steps to safeguard its rights.
S S
[D] reserves all of its legal rights accordingly.”
T T
U U
V V

- 8 -
A A
B 15. In response, B&M wrote to HSF on 7 January 2019, as B
follows:
C C
“…
D D
Whilst reserving all of [C]’s rights in this regard, we would
observe that the procedure laid out at sections 8.2 and 14 of
E
the Cooperation Agreement, and the potential engagement
E
of the respective Chief Executive Officers does not concern
[C]’s Directors.
F F
[D]’s direct communication with [C]’s Directors in all
G circumstances is neither appropriate nor productive. G
We request that all further correspondence on this matter be
H directed to us or if pursuant to Clause 14.2 of the H
Agreement be addressed to the Chief Executive Officer of
our client, copying us.”
I I
J 16. There was no further correspondence from D. Neither party J
referred the dispute to the respective Chief Executive Officers with a
K K
view to resolving the dispute through negotiation.
L L
17. On 18 April 2019, D issued a notice referring the dispute to
M arbitration under Clause 14.3. In response, C claimed, among other M
things, that the arbitral tribunal did not have jurisdiction to entertain the
N N
dispute due to the absence of a request for negotiation under Clauses 14.2
O and 14.3. O
P 18. An arbitral tribunal of three arbitrators (“the Tribunal”) was P
formed. They decided to deal with C’s objection on jurisdiction and the
Q Q
issue of liability together, leaving the issue of quantum to be addressed, if
R necessary, at a later stage. After a hearing which took place in Hong R
Kong on 2 and 3 January 2020, the Tribunal issued an award (“the Partial
S S
Award”) on 21 April 2020, finding in favour of D:
T T
U U
V V

- 9 -
A A
B (1) In relation to the issue of jurisdiction, the Tribunal held that B
the first sentence in Clause 14.2 mandatorily required the
C C
parties to attempt in good faith to resolve any dispute by
D
negotiation, but the reference of dispute to the respective
D
Chief Executive Officers mentioned in the second sentence
E E
of Clause 14.2 was optional.
F F
(2) The Tribunal further held that the condition in Clause 14.3,
G ie the dispute could not be resolved within 60 business days G
of a party’s request in writing for such negotiation, referred
H H
to a request for negotiation under the first sentence of Clause
I
14.2, and that condition had been fulfilled by D by the
I
December Letter.
J J
(3) The Tribunal accordingly rejected C’s objection on
K K
jurisdiction, and proceeded to find that C had breached
L
Clause 4.7 and was liable to pay damages to D in an amount
L
to be assessed.
M M
N THE JUDGMENT N
O O
19. On 21 May 2020, C issued an originating summons seeking
a declaration that the Partial Award was made without jurisdiction and
P P
not binding on C, and an order that the Partial Award be set aside under
Q Q
s 81, which, so far as material, states as follows:
R R
“Article 34 of UNCITRAL model Law (Application for
setting aside as exclusive recourse against arbitral
S award) S
(1) Article 34 of the UNCITRAL Model Law, the text of
T which is set out below, has effect subject to section 13(5) - T
U U
V V

- 10 -
A A
B ‘Article 34. Application for setting aside as B
exclusive recourse against arbitral award
C
(1) Recourse to a court against an arbitral award
C
may be made only by an application for
setting aside in accordance with paragraphs
D D
(2) and (3) of this article.
E (2) An arbitral award may be set aside by the E
court specified in article 6 only if:
F (a) the party making the application F
furnishes proof that:
G … G
(iii) the award deals with a dispute
H
not contemplated by or not
H
falling within the terms of the
submission to arbitration, or
I I
contains decisions on matters
beyond the scope of the
J submission to arbitration, J
provided that, if the decisions
on matters submitted to
K K
arbitration can be separated
from those not so submitted,
L only that part of the award L
which contains decisions on
matters not submitted to
M M
arbitration may be set aside;
or
N N
(iv) the composition of the arbitral
tribunal or the arbitral
O procedure was not in O
accordance with the
agreement of the parties,
P
unless such agreement was in
P
conflict with a provision of
this Law from which the
Q Q
parties cannot derogate, or,
failing such agreement, was
R not in accordance with this R
Law …’”
S S
20. At the hearing of the originating summons before the Judge
T T
on 24 February 2021, it was common ground that the first sentence in
U U
V V

- 11 -
A A
B Clause 14.3 meant that it was a condition precedent to any reference to B
arbitration that there should have been a request in writing for negotiation
C C
and that the dispute nevertheless could not be resolved amicably within
D
60 business days. The parties differed, however, on what the condition
D
meant:
E E
(1) C contended that the condition referred to the giving of a
F F
written notice to have the dispute referred to the Chief
G G
Executive Officers for resolution, as referred to in the second
H sentence of Clause 14.2, and no such written notice was H
given.
I I
(2) On the other hand, D contended that the condition was
J J
satisfied by a written request to negotiate in good faith, as
K referred to in the first sentence of Clause 14.2, and that it had K
made the requisite request by the December Letter.
L L
(3) D further contended that the question of whether the
M M
condition precedent had been fulfilled was a question of
N N
“admissibility” rather than “jurisdiction”, and as such the
O court should not interfere with the Tribunal’s decision on O
that question.
P P
21. The Judge identified two questions which arose for
Q Q
consideration (§26 of the Judgment):
R R
(1) The primary question: is the question whether D complied
S S
with the dispute resolution procedure set out in Clause 14.2
T of the Agreement a question of the admissibility of the claim, T
U U
V V

- 12 -
A A
B or a question of the tribunal’s jurisdiction, and does that B
question fall within s 81(1)?
C C
(2) The secondary question (only if the primary question is
D D
answered in C’s favour): what is the condition precedent to
E E
arbitration on the proper construction of the Agreement, and
was the condition fulfilled by the December Letter?
F F
G G
22. In respect of the primary question, the Judge held that:
H H
(1) The court may review the Tribunal’s decision on the
I standard of “correctness” and decide the question de novo if I
the question of whether D complied with the dispute
J J
resolution procedure set out in Clause 14.2 is a true question
K of “jurisdiction” properly falling within Art 34 (§28 of the K
Judgment).
L L
(2) The distinction between “jurisdiction” and “admissibility” is
M M
recognized both in court decisions in the United Kingdom,
N N
Singapore and United States, as well as in various academic
works (§§30-36 and 37-42 of the Judgment).
O O
P (3) Although the Ordinance does not in terms draw a distinction P
between jurisdiction and admissibility, it may properly be
Q Q
relied upon to inform the construction and application of
R s 81 (§43 of the Judgment). R
S S
T T
U U
V V

- 13 -
A A
B (4) C’s objection in the present case is one going to the B
admissibility of the claim, rather than the jurisdiction of the
C C
arbitral tribunal (§53 of the Judgment).
D D
(5) As such, the objection does not fall under Art 34(2)(a)(iii)
E E
(§54 of the Judgment).
F F
(6) Neither is Art 34(2)(a)(iv) applicable to C’s objection
G because that provision concerns the way in which the G
arbitration was conducted, but not contractual procedures
H H
preceding the arbitration, or pre-arbitration dispute
I resolution procedures such as those provided in the I
Agreement (§§55-57 of the Judgment).
J J
(7) Having reached the conclusion that C’s objection does not
K K
fall within either Art 34(2)(a)(iii) or (iv), it becomes
L L
unnecessary to deal with the secondary question (§58 of the
Judgment).
M M
N N
THE PRESENT APPEAL
O O
23. On 8 August 2021, the Judge granted C leave to appeal the
P Judgment, on the basis that multi-tiered dispute resolution clauses are not P
uncommon, and the question of the proper approach to an application to
Q Q
set aside an arbitral award on the ground that certain prior requisite steps
R envisaged by such a clause have not been undertaken and that the arbitral R
tribunal consequently lacks jurisdiction is a subject matter of some
S S
general significance to arbitration law in Hong Kong.
T T
U U
V V

- 14 -
A A
B 24. On 13 August 2021, C filed a Notice of Appeal pursuant to B
the leave granted by the Judge. The Notice of Appeal contains three
C C
grounds. The first two grounds challenge the Judge’s conclusion that C’s
D
objection to the Partial Award does not fall within Art 34(2)(a)(iii) or (iv):
D
E
(1) Under Ground 1, C contends that the Judge erred in holding
E
that it had failed to show that the Partial Award dealt with a
F F
dispute not falling within the “terms of the submission to
G G
arbitration” under Art 34(2)(a)(iii).
H H
(2) Under Ground 2, C contends that the Judge erred in holding
that Art 34(2)(a)(iv) was apt to refer to the way in which the
I I
arbitration was conducted but not to the contractual
J J
procedures preceding the arbitration.
K K
25. Ground 3 concerns the question which the Judge considered
L L
he did not need to deal with (ie the construction of Clauses 14.2 and 14.3,
and whether the relevant condition precedent was fulfilled).
M M
N N
GROUND 1: WHETHER PARTIAL AWARD DEALT WITH A DISPUTE
NOT FALLING WITHIN THE TERMS OF THE SUBMISSION TO
O ARBITRATION UNDER ART 34(2)(a)(iii)? O
P (i) C’s argument P
Q 26. Mr Benjamin Yu, SC (on behalf of C) argues that the Judge Q
erred in 2 respects:
R R
(1) Assuming that there exists a distinction between questions of
S S
“admissibility” and “jurisdiction” and that only the latter
T T
U U
V V

- 15 -
A A
B falls within Art 34(2)(a)(iii), C’s challenge is jurisdictional B
in nature2.
C C
(2) The distinction between “admissibility” and “jurisdiction”
D D
ought not to be adopted since it is not found in Art
E
34(2)(a)(iii) or (iv), and the question should simply be
E
whether the Partial Award dealt with a dispute “not
F F
contemplated by or not falling within the terms of the
G G
submission to arbitration” (as Art 34(2)(a)(iii) states), and
whether “the arbitral procedure was not in accordance with
H H
the agreement of the parties” (as Art 34(2)(a)(iv) states)3.
I I
27. Pausing here, it is clear from the Notice of Appeal that
J J
Ground 1 is concerned only with Art 34(2)(a)(iii), while Art 34(2)(a)(iv)
is dealt with under Ground 2. Accordingly, Mr Yu’s argument in respect
K K
of Art 34(2)(a)(iv) will be dealt with in the next section of this judgment
L L
relating to Ground 2. In respect of Ground 1, we shall first consider the
question of the true construction of Art 34(2)(a)(iii), before dealing with
M M
the question of whether C’s objection to the arbitration in the present case
N N
should properly be characterized as an objection going to the jurisdiction
O of the tribunal rather than the admissibility of the claim. O
P (ii) The distinction between “admissibility” and “jurisdiction” P
Q 28. For the purpose of determining the permissible scope of Q
challenge to an award made by an arbitral tribunal under Art 34(2)(a)(iii)
R R
(or similar provisions), the distinction drawn between objections to
S S
2 See §11 of the Skeleton Submissions for the Appellant dated 29 March 2022.
T 3 See §28 of the Skeleton Submissions for the Appellant. T
U U
V V

- 16 -
A A
B admissibility and jurisdiction is well recognised in both case law and B
academic writings.
C C
Case law
D D
29. UK: s 67(1) of the Arbitration Act 1996 permits a party to
E E
arbitral proceedings to challenge an award of an arbitral tribunal as to its
F F
“substantive jurisdiction”, while s 30(1) of the 1996 Act provides that an
arbitral tribunal may rule on its own substantive jurisdiction as to (a)
G G
whether there is a valid arbitration agreement, (b) whether the tribunal is
H H
properly constituted, and (c) what matters have been submitted to
I arbitration in accordance with the arbitration agreement. I
J 30. In Republic of Sierra Leone v SL Mining Ltd [2021] Bus J
LR 704, the relevant dispute resolution clause provided that “[t]he parties
K K
shall in good faith endeavour to reach an amicable settlement of all
L differences of opinions or dispute which may arise between them in L
respect of the execution performance interpretation or termination of the
M M
agreement”, and that “[i]n the event that the parties shall be unable to
N reach an amicable settlement within a period of 3 (three) months from a N
written notice by one party to the other specifying the nature of the
O O
dispute and seeking an amicable settlement, either party may submit the
P matter to the exclusive jurisdiction of a Board of 3 (three) Arbitrators …”. P
An arbitral award was challenged on the ground that the 3-month
Q Q
negotiation period had not yet expired by the time of the request for
R arbitration, and thus the arbitrators were without jurisdiction. Sir Michael R
Burton (sitting as a High Court judge) held that there was a distinction
S S
between a challenge that a claim was not admissible before arbitrators
T (admissibility) and a challenge that the arbitrators had no jurisdiction to T
U U
V V

- 17 -
A A
B hear a claim (jurisdiction), and that only the latter was available to a party B
under s 67, observing that such distinction was seemingly first drawn out
C C
judicially by Butcher J in Obrascon Huarte Lain SA (trading as OHL
D
International) v Qatar Foundation for Education, Science and
D
Community Development [2020] EWHC 1643 (Comm), PAO Tatneft v
E E
Ukraine [2018] 1 WLR 5947, and Republic of Korea v Dayyani [2020]
F F
Bur LR 884. Sir Michael further held that an objection based on an
alleged prematurity of a request for arbitration such as that before him
G G
was one going to the admissibility of the claim rather than the jurisdiction
H H
of the tribunal. At §§16-21 of his judgment, Sir Michael stated as follows:
I I
“[16] The international authorities are plainly
overwhelmingly in support of a case that a challenge
J such as the present does not go to jurisdiction, but at J
the end of the day the matter comes down at English
law to an issue as to whether the question of
K
prematurity falls within section 30(1)(c) of the 1996
K
Act. I do not accept Mr Lightfoot’s case that much
depends upon the precise wording of the clause. I do
L L
not see that there would be any difference between
